operating_state: Net revenue reached $16.4 billion in 2021, increasing 68% from $9.8 billion in 2020, driven by Computing and Graphics segment growth and Enterprise, Embedded and Semi-Custom segment expansion. Growth reflected 57% higher average selling prices from premium Ryzen, Radeon and AMD Instinct product mix, partially offset by lower unit shipments due to strategic focus on higher-end products in tight supply conditions.
cost_structure: Gross margin improved to 48% from 45% in 2020, primarily driven by richer mix of EPYC, Radeon and Ryzen processor sales. Operating expenses increased significantly driven by higher headcount, increased annual employee incentives, and expanded go-to-market activities. Manufacturing costs remained pressured by reliance on third-party foundries, creating yield-dependent per-unit cost variability and price protection agreement margin pressure.
material_events: Board approved $4 billion stock repurchase program in May 2021, deploying $1.8 billion to repurchase 16.7 million shares during the year. AMD amended wafer supply agreement with GLOBALFOUNDRIES securing minimum annual capacity allocation through 2025 with corresponding wafer purchase targets and prepayment obligations while eliminating exclusivity commitments. Multiple high-performance product launches occurred including AMD Ryzen 5000 Series Mobile Processors, Radeon RX 6000 series graphics cards, and EPYC 7003 Series CPUs.
macro_exposures: International sales represented 72% of net revenue, maintaining significant exposure to global economic and political conditions, with worldwide manufacturing footprint concentrated in Asia creating exposure to regional instability and trade policy changes. US-China trade tensions create ongoing tariff and export control risks, with Entity List designations affecting Chinese joint venture operations. COVID-19 pandemic continued disrupting global supply chains and workforce productivity.
forward_outlook: Xilinx acquisition expected to close in first quarter 2022 subject to regulatory approvals, establishing AMD as high-performance computing industry leader with operational synergies and enhanced product innovation capabilities. Company secured long-term supply capacity through 2025 to support future revenue growth, with management maintaining remaining authorization under stock repurchase program.
uncertainty_profile: Xilinx merger completion depends on regulatory approvals that may impose conditions or prevent closing entirely, with potential termination fees. Debt agreements impose operational restrictions including investment limitations, merger constraints, and cross-default provisions that could trigger acceleration of outstanding obligations. Complex supply chain dependencies on sole suppliers create operational risks, while cryptocurrency mining demand volatility introduces unpredictable GPU revenue fluctuations.
